A corpus carries information only where its content is both mutually consistent and non-redundant; a synthetic corpus with no persistent coordinating world-state has no source of consistency, so a generator is forced toward contradiction or genericness.

Real corpora get mutual consistency for free because every document describes the same coordinating referent, reality. A synthetic corpus generated by prompting an LLM independently per document has no such coordinator: if the LLM tries to be specific it risks contradicting other documents, so it drifts toward safe, generic, redundant content instead. This is a mechanistic account of the corpus monoculture observed empirically.

This principle explains why increasing prompt variety and lexical diversity controls did not fully fix repetitiveness: the ceiling is not the prompt, it is the absence of a shared state that makes specific, varied content also consistent. It motivates constructing a persistent world-state (node 36) as the coordination mechanism, so that documents can be specific and varied while remaining consistent with each other, because they are all renderings of the same underlying structure rather than independent LLM improvisations.
